major_tropes: The Detective/Investigator Who Goes Too Far
The Crime That Is Bigger Than It Appears
The Witness Who Cannot Be Protected
The Clock Ticking Toward Another Victim
The System That Is Part of the Problem
plot_template: A detective or investigator takes on a case that seems containable and discovers it threads through institutions, powerful people, or a serial threat that requires breaking every rule they have. The investigation costs them personally — relationships, reputation, safety — and the resolution forces a choice about what justice actually means.
character_archetypes: The Obsessive Detective With a Personal Connection to the Case
The Victim Who Is More Than a Victim
The Colleague Whose Loyalty Is Being Tested
The Criminal Who Has a Coherent (If Monstrous) Logic
world_building_notes: Police procedure must be accurate enough to feel real but subordinated to character. The institutional setting (police department, DA office, newsroom) has its own politics and compromises. Crime scenes, forensics, and investigation specifics should be precise. The city or town is a character — its geography, demographics, and power structures shape who gets justice and who doesn't.
emotional_core: Justice vs. law, the personal cost of obsession, moral compromise in service of the right outcome, what we owe the dead
